content:
# Joshua Chapter 18 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 241-242 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_018_page_001.jpg`

---

*[Land Divided at Shiloh — Twenty-eight verses of survey and settlement. The whole community gathers at Shiloh and sets up the Tent of Meeting. Seven tribes still have no land. Joshua rebukes their hesitation, sends out surveyors, and casts lots before YHWH. Then the chapter traces Benjamin's territory in careful detail — wedged between Judah and Joseph, a narrow strip running from the Jordan to the hill country, containing twenty-six cities including Jericho, Bethel, Gibeon, and the Jebusite city that will one day be called Jerusalem.]*

---

**1** The whole community of the Sons of Israel gathered at Shiloh and set up the Tent of Meeting there. The land lay subdued before them — **2** but seven tribes among the Sons of Israel had still not received their inheritance.

**3** So Joshua said to the Sons of Israel, "How long are you going to put off going in to take possession of the land that YHWH, the God of your fathers, has given you? **4** Choose three men from each tribe. I will send them out, and they will go through the land and write up a description of it, territory by territory, for their inheritance. Then they will come back to me. **5** They are to divide the land into seven portions. Judah will stay in his territory in the south, and the house of Joseph will stay in their territory in the north. **6** You are to write up a description of the land in seven portions and bring it here to me. Then I will cast lots for you here before YHWH our God.

**7** "The Levites have no share among you, because the priesthood of YHWH is their inheritance. And Gad, Reuben, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have already received their inheritance east of the Jordan, which Moses, the servant of YHWH, gave them."

**8** The men got up and set out. Joshua gave these instructions to those going to survey the land: "Go, walk through the land, write up a description, and come back to me. Then I will cast lots for you here before YHWH in Shiloh."

**9** So the men went out, passed through the land, and wrote up a description of it — city by city, in seven portions — on a scroll. Then they returned to Joshua at the camp in Shiloh.

**10** Joshua cast lots for them at Shiloh before YHWH, and there he divided the land among the Sons of Israel according to their tribal divisions.

**11** The lot for the tribe of the sons of Benjamin came up, clan by clan. Their allotted territory fell between the sons of Judah and the sons of Joseph.

**12** On the north side, their boundary started at the Jordan, went up along the northern slope of Jericho, climbed westward into the hill country, and ended at the wilderness of Beth-aven.

**13** From there the boundary crossed over to Luz — that is, Bethel — along its southern slope. Then it went down to Ataroth-addar, on the mountain south of Lower Beth-horon.

**14** The boundary curved and turned southward on the western side, from the mountain facing Beth-horon, and ended at Kiriath-baal — that is, Kiriath-jearim — a city belonging to the sons of Judah. This was the western side.

**15** The southern side started from the edge of Kiriath-jearim, and the boundary went westward, coming out at the spring of the Waters of Nephtoah.

**16** Then the boundary went down to the foot of the mountain facing the Valley of Ben-hinnom, in the Valley of the Rephaim to the north. It descended into the Hinnom Valley along the southern slope of the Jebusite ridge and went down to En-rogel.

**17** It curved northward and came out at En-shemesh, then went on to Geliloth, which faces the Ascent of Adummim. It descended to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.

**18** It passed along the northern side of the slope facing the Arabah and went down into the Arabah.

**19** Then the boundary passed along the northern side of Beth-hoglah and ended at the northern bay of the Salt Sea, at the southern end of the Jordan. This was the southern boundary.

**20** The Jordan formed the boundary on the east side. This was the inheritance of the sons of Benjamin — its borders on every side — according to their clans.

**21** The cities belonging to the tribe of the sons of Benjamin, by their clans, were: Jericho, Beth-hoglah, Emek-keziz, **22** Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, Bethel, **23** Avvim, Parah, Ophrah, **24** Chephar-ammoni, Ophni, and Geba — twelve cities with their villages.

**25** Gibeon, Ramah, Beeroth, **26** Mizpeh, Chephirah, Mozah, **27** Rekem, Irpeel, Taralah, **28** Zela, Eleph, and the Jebusite city — that is, Jerusalem — Gibeah, and Kiriath — fourteen cities with their villages.

This was the inheritance of the sons of Benjamin, clan by clan.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **v1 — Shiloh as the new center:** The move from Gilgal to Shiloh marks a turning point. By setting up the Tent of Meeting here, Israel transitions from a mobile military camp to a settled worship center. Shiloh will remain the central sanctuary until the time of Samuel. The Hebrew says the land was "subdued" (nikhbeshah) before them — a passive participle conveying that the military campaigns have accomplished their purpose.

2. **v3 — "How long are you going to put off...":** The Hebrew mitrappim (Hitpael of raphah) carries real bite — Joshua is accusing the remaining tribes of going slack, being negligent. PNEUMA renders this as "put off going in" to capture the procrastination nuance in natural English, rather than the more archaic "be slack."

3. **v5 — Judah and Joseph hold their ground:** The Hebrew "shall stand upon his border" means these tribes retain their already-assigned territories as fixed points while the remaining land is surveyed and divided. The house of Joseph refers collectively to Ephraim and the western half of Manasseh.

4. **v7 — Why only seven tribes:** Joshua explains the math. Judah and Joseph already have land (south and north). Gad, Reuben, and eastern Manasseh received theirs from Moses east of the Jordan. The Levites receive no territorial allotment. That accounts for the other five, leaving seven without land.

5. **v9 — The first land survey:** The Hebrew sepher ("scroll") indicates this was a written document — a remarkably bureaucratic act for the period. Three men per tribe surveyed the remaining territory and recorded it systematically by city, creating what may be the earliest described cadastral survey in ancient Near Eastern literature.

6. **v11 — Benjamin's strategic position:** Benjamin's territory falls between Judah (the major southern tribe) and Joseph (the major northern bloc). This narrow but strategically vital strip will later become the contested ground between the northern and southern kingdoms.

7. **v12 — Beth-aven:** Literally "House of Wickedness" (or "House of Nothing"). This was a place near Bethel in the wilderness. Some scholars suggest it was a polemical name for Bethel itself after the northern sanctuary was established there.

8. **v16 — Valley of Ben-hinnom:** Literally "Valley of the Son of Hinnom" — the geographic name that later becomes Gehinnom (Gehenna). Located south of Jerusalem, it would become infamous as a site of child sacrifice under later kings and eventually a metaphor for judgment.

9. **v19 — "the northern bay of the Salt Sea":** The Hebrew uses leshon, literally "tongue," to describe a protruding inlet or bay of the Dead Sea. This vivid geographic metaphor appears repeatedly in biblical boundary descriptions.

10. **v28 — The city-count problem:** The text says "fourteen cities" for the second group, but counting the names yields either thirteen or fifteen depending on how certain compound names are read. The Masoretic punctuation of the Leningrad Codex may suggest that "Zela-Eleph" is one compound name and "Gibeah-Kiriath" another, which would yield fourteen. This is a longstanding textual question with no certain resolution.
